Farn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouprrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June9,2006
CraigA. Steele
Richards Watson Gershon
Attomey atLaw
355 South Grand Avenue,40e Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3101
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4,-06-091
Dear Mr. Steele:
This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Councilmember
Mary AwtLrttz, regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 'Act,,).I
QUESTIONS
1. Would pa5rments by member cities of the San Gabriel Valley Council of
Govemments ("SGVCOG") and the Gateway cities council of Governments
("GCCOG"), referred to collectively herein as the "coGS," for the services of a
employee or consultant to provide staff services to Councilmember Lutz, solely to assist
with her services on the Los Angeles Regional Water euality Control Board
("RWQCB"), be considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember?
2. Would the assignment of a city employee to provide staff services to
CouncilmemberLttz by the City of Monrovia ("City') (reimbursed in whole or in part by
the member cities of the COGS) solely to assist with her services on the RWeCB be
considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember?
3. Would Councilmember L:utz be required to disclose the payments made for the
provision of staff services?
4. Would Councilmember Lttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing the COGS,
decision to provide employee/consultant or the City's decision to provide employee with
reimbursement from the COGS?
t Governm"nt Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appearat Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
qs:El


File No. 4-06-091
Page No. 2
5. Would Councilmember Ltttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing a decision of
the RWQCB regarding the coGS, any member city, and/or the citt'l
CONCLUSIONS
1 and 2. Based upon the facts submitted, the payments in question will be neither
reportable contributions nor gifts to the councilmernber.
3. While the payments in question would not be considered a contribution or a
gift, there is still limited reporting of these payments. CouncilmemberLytzmust report
any member city, the SGVCOG, the GCCOG, andlor the City if any of these parties
make payments aggregating $5,000 or more in a calend ar year.
4 and 5. Based on the facts submitted, Councilmember Lutz would not have an
economic interest in the COGS, the member cities, or the City as the payments in
question would not be considered a gift. CouncilmemberLttz,by virtue of the
payrnents, would not be prohibited by the Act from making, participating in making or
influencing a govenrmental decision, provided there is no personal financial effect on her
or members of her immediate family.
FACTS
Councilmember Lutz is one of five members of the Monrovia City Council. In
2005, CouncilmemberLutz was appointed to the Los Angeles Regional Water euality
Control Board. Many of the matters, which come before the RWQCB, have far-reaching
policy implications and involve complex elements of engineeringand/orhydrology and
environmental science. Individual business items on the RWQCB's monthly -".t1rg
agendas generate hundreds of pages of staff reports and written testimony frlm the piUti"
and the regulated community. Members of the RWQCB do not have specifically
designated staff assistants and are not provided funds to engage staff services.
CouncilmemberLutz spends many hours each month studying agenda materials and
preparing for each RWQCB meeting. CouncilmemberLatzfeels that she needs the
occasional assistance of a professional experienced in the subject matter of the RWeCB
to help her prepare for the RWQCB meetings and to adequately represent the interest of
local government in Los Angeles County.
The City is one of 30 members of the SGVCOG, a joint powers authority and
public agency formed pursuant to Government Code section 6500 et seq. Anadditional
27 cities make up the GCCOG, a similar joint powers authority. Regional water quality
is a significant focus of the work of the COGS, the members of which are regulat.O Uy-
the RWQCB. The COGS have offered to employ, atthe expense of their city membeis, a
part-time consuitant who would serve as CouncilmemberLutz'staff member for


File No. A-06-091
Page No. 3
RWQCB matters. The coGS would not solicit or accept any private or non-
goveflrmental funding to pay for this staff assistant.
As an alternative to the proposal outlined above, the City is considering hiring or
reassigning a staff member to assist Councilmemb er Lutz with her RWeCB duties.
Under this alternative, the City would pay the staff member directly but would be
reimbursed by the member cities of the COGS. Again, no private or non-governmental
funding would be used to pay for this staff assistant.
Under either proposal, the staff member would work solely on RWeCB matters
and not on city council, personal, or political matters.
{.NALYSIS
Ouestions I and 2
Generally, when a payment is made to an elected official, such as a city council
member, and that official does not provide something of equal value in exchange, it is
considered either a contribution or a glft. (See sections g2015 and, g202g.)
The Act defines a "contribution" as "a payment, forgiveness of a loan, a payment
of a loan by a third PartY, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that fulI and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear fromihe
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes.,, (Section gzol5)2
The definition of "contribution" was amend ed in 1997 with respect to pa5zments
made at the behest of candidates. The stated pu{pose of the amendment is set forth in the
Senate Floor analysis as follows:
"This bill recognizes that elected officeholders engage in
governmental, legislative and charitable activities which are neither
'campaign' activities nor 'personal' activities. payments made by others
to assist in the conduct of such governmental, legislative, or charitable
activities, even 'at the behest of an elected officeholder are neither ,glfts,
nor 'contributions' and should not be subject to limits. The bill does,
however, require public disclosure of these payments once a threshold is
met and exceeded." (senate Rules committee senate Floor Analysis of
sB 124 (4t30te7).)
2 Section 82007 defines a "candidate" in pertinent part, as an individual who is listed on the ballot
for elective office. An individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her status as a candidate until
such time as that status is terminated pursuant to section 84214. As an elected official, Councilmember
Lutz has current status as a candidate for purposes of the Act and payrnents received may still be
considered contributions.


File No. 4-06-091
Page No. 4
As amended, section 52015b)Q) states that apayment made for a political
purpose is a "contribution" if it is received by or made at the "behest of a candidate.,, A
payment is made at the "behest" of a candidate whenever it is made 'lrnder the control or
at the direction of in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at the
request or suggestion of or with the express, prior consent of' a candidate. (Section
82015; regulation 78225.7, copy enclosed.) Because Councilmember Lutz is an elected
official and continues to have status as a candidate under section 82007,section
82015(b)Q) is applicable to anypayments made at the'behest" of Councilmember Lutz.
Under section 82015(b)(2), a payment is made for ,.political purposes,, and
consequentially a "contribution" unless the criteria in eithersubp*ugruph(A) or (B)
below are satisfied:
"(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the
candidate.
"(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the
payment was made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for
elective office. The following tlpes ofpayments are presumed to be for
purposes unrelated to a candidate's candidacy for elective office:
"(i) A paym.ent made principaly forpersonal purposes, in which
case it may be considered a gift under the provisions of Sict ion 8202g.
Payments that are otherwise subject to the limits of Section 86203 are
presumed to be principally for personal purposes.
"(ii) A payment made by a state, rocal, or federar govemmental
agency or by a nonprofit organization that is exempt fromtaxation under
Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
"(iii) A payrnent not covered by clause (i), made principally for
legislative, govemmental, or charitable pu{poses, in which "u., it i,
neither a gift nor a contribution ....,,
From the facts submitted, Councilmember Lutzhas indicated that she .heeds the
occasional assistance of a professional experienced in the subject matter of the
RWQCB...." The COGS and consequentially the member cities have offered to pay for
that assistance. All indications are that the COGS made such offers in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with Councilmember Lutz or at her request or
suggestion. Accordingly, we conclude that the payments to provide an
employee/consultant to assist Councilmember Ltilz *.r" -ud" at her behest. (Section
8201 5; regulation 1 8225.7 .)


File No. A-06-091
Page No. 5
You have presented two alternatives. Under the first altemative, the member
cities will pay for an additionally COG employee or a consultant to assist with
CouncilmemberLttz' services on the RWQCB. Under the second alternative, the City
will hire an employee to assist with Councilmember Lutz' services on the nWeCB and
the member cities of the COGS would reimburse the City. You have also provided the
facts that the employee/consultant will work solely on RWQCB matters and that no
funding will come from private or non-governmental entities. While the alternatives you
have proposed differ in form, the legal obligations are the same under both altematives
with the exception of identifring the payors of the payments, for reporting pu{poses, as
discussed below.
Based upon the facts submitted, the payments from the COGS, the mernber cities,
or the City, all local governmental agencies, will be received by ao employee/consultant
to provide services solely related to Councilmember Lutz' duties on the RWeCB. While
the occasional assistance of a professional experienced in the subject matter oith.
RWQCB would undoubtedly aid CouncilmemberLurrzin performing her duties, the
principal purpose of the assistance would be aiding and facilitatirg gi,,"--ental duties,
a govemmental purpose, and not principally for a personal putpose.
As the payments will be made by a local govemmental agency,the payments are
presumed to be for a purpose unrelated to the candidate's candidacy for et"Lt"a office and
are not considered contributions under section 82015@)Q)@Xii).3 Additionally, section
82015@)(2XBXiii) states that apayment by any entity, not just govemmental entities and
nonprofit orgarizations, made at the behest of an elected official principally for
legislative, govemmental, or charitable purposes will not be considered a contribution or
a gift as long as section 820r5(b)(2xB)(i) does not apply. (KampAdvice Letter, No.
A-97-63(a).) Therefore, we conclude that as the payments are principally for a
govefiImental purpose, the payments are neither contributions nor gifts under section
820L5(b)Q)@Xiii).. Please note that even though payments are neither contributions nor
gifts to CouncilmemberLltz, it is still important to caution that this conclusion isfact
sensitive.
Ouestion 3
Even though the payments made at Councilmember Lutz'behest do not appear to
be contributions or gifts, there is still limited reporting of these payrnents as the payments
will be made principally for a governmental purpose. (Section szbrs6;(2)(B)(iii), copy
3 We note that lgction 8201 s(bX2XB)(ii) also applies to payments by a non profit organization
orgarized under section 501(c)(3) ofthe Internal Revenue Code.
a Section s2015(b)(2)id)(iii; proviaes that payments which are not principally for personal
putpose made principally for a legislative, governmental, or charitabl" pr.por" are neitieru gift ro. u
contribution. As the classification of the payments is controlled by seciion 8201 5(bX2XBXfi), no further
analysis of the palments is warranted under section 82028. (See Sutton Advice I-eier,'No.'a -OS-ZSA;
Taylor AdviceLetter, No. 4-03-085; Sellers Advice Letter,No. I-00-138; and SchmritAdvice Letter, No.
A-97-14s.)


File No. A-06-091
Page No. 6
enclosed.)s The payments to the employee/consultant hired for Councilme mber Lutz
must be reported as follows:
"... payments of this type that are made at the behest of a candidate
who is an elected officer shall be reported within 30 days following the
date on which the payment or payments equal or exceed five thousand
dollars ($ 5,000) in the aggregate from the same source in the same
calendar year in which they are made. The report shall be filed by the
elected officer with the elected officer's agency and shall be a public
record subject to inspection and copying pursuant to the provisions of
subdivision (a) of section 81008. The report shall contain the following
information: name of payor, address of payor, amount of the payment,
date or dates the payment or payments were made, the name and address
ofthe payee, a briefdescription ofthe goods or services provided or
purchased, if any, and a description of the specific purpose or event for
which the payment or payments were made. once the five thousand
dollars ($ 5,000) aggregate threshold from a single source has been
reached for a calendar year, all payments for the calendar year made by
that source must be disclosed within 30 days after the date the threshold
was reached or the payment was made, whichever occurs later. within 30
days after receipt of the report, state agencies shall forward a copy of these
reports to the Fair Political Practices Commission, and local agencies shall
forward a copy of these reports to the officer with whom elected officers
of that agency file their campaign statements.,, (Section
8201s(b)(2xBxiii).)
Under your first alternative, the member cities will pay the COGS to hire the
anployee/consultant. Under your second alternative, the City will pay aiemployee and
be reimbursed by the member cities. Under the second alternative,lt is unclear whether
the member cities will make payments through the COGS or directly to the City. These
scenarios create a question as to identi$ring the payors, which must be identified when
reporting the payments.
The Commission has advised that pa5rments from a nonprofit organization
aggtegating $5,000 or more in a calendar year must be reported where tLe organization
has made the payments from funds collected from individual donors. AdditiJnally,
payments from the individual donors must be reported if a donor has made payments
s The Commission has advised that where payments are made principally for legislative,
goverunental, or charitable purposes and equal or exceed $5,000 in the aggregate in a .ul"ndry year, then
reporting obligations will occur under section 82015(bX2XBXiii). (Kamp Advice Letter, supra; see also
Garvey Advice Letter, No. ,{-99-250.) Similarly, as the payments made at Councilmember^Lutz, behest
are from a govemmental agency, the payments will not be considered contributions or gifts, but the
payments are subject to the reporting obligations of section 92015(bX2XBXiii).


File No. 4-06-091
Page No. 7
aggregating $5,000 or more in a calendar year to the nonprofit organization. (See Sutton
Advice Letter, supra; and Taylor Advice Letter, supra.)
Accordingly, Councilmember Lutz must report all parties who make payments
aggregating $5,000 or more in a calendar year. For example, if the member cities each
make payments aggregating$5,000 or more to the COGS; and the COGS use these funds
to make reimbursement payments to the City forpaylng acity employee to assist with
councilmember Lutz' services on the RwecB, each mernb er city,the sGVCoG, the
GCCOG, and the city are all considered payors and must be reported.
Ouestion 4
Additionally, you ask whether CouncilmemberLutzwould be prohibited from
making, patticipating in making, or influencing any govemmental decision under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act as a result of the coGS providing an
employee/consultant to assist with Councilmember Lutz' service on the niVqCn.6
Section 871OO prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or rnor" of the
public official's economic interests. (Section g7L}3;regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whJher an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given goveflrmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) However, your questio, concirns only step three of the
analysis and entails only the determination of whether Councilmember Lutz has an
economic interest, which may be disqualifying.
step Three: what are the 66economic interests', of councilmember Lutz?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 1g703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition of
management (section 87 1 03 (d); regulation 1 3703. 1 (b));
6 Since the councilmember would not be receiving reportable gifts, it is not necessary to address
whether these payments are reportable under section g7207.


o
File No. 4-06-091
Page No. 8
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section SZ1O:1";;
regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(e); regulation
18703.4);
a
a
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (section87103;
regulation 18703.5).
You have solelypresented the question of whether Councilmember Lutz would
have an economic interest in the COGS, the member cities and,lor the City should the
COGS, the member cities and/or the City provide an employee/consultani to assist with
CouncilmernberLutz' services on the RWeCB.
The only economic interests indicated from your account of the facts, is a possible
economic interest in the COGS, the member cities, or the City as a source of eifts and
Councilmemb er Llutz' economic interest in her personal finances.7 As previoirsly
discussed, the payments for an ernployee/consultant hired by a govemmental agency to
assist with Councilmember Lutz' services on the RWQCB would not be considereda
gift. Consequently, CouncilmemberLutzwould not have an economic interest in the
COGS, the member cities, or the City as a source of gifts under the facts provided.
As to the personal financial effects, a goveflrment decision has a personal
financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section SZtO:;
regulation 18703.5.) If facts suggest any financial effect on Couniilmember Lutz,
personal finances from a governmental decision, Councilmember Lrttz' economic interest
in her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation
187045.) A financial effect on an official's personal finances is considered material iq
"it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." (Regulation 18705.5(a).) If it is reasonabiy
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect of at least $250 in a l2-month period on
councilmemberLutz'personal finances resulting from her acceptance of the
employee/consultant's assistance, a conflict of interest violation.lvould occur.
7 As discrrssed above, an official's "personal finances" include the personal finances ofthe
official's immediate family. (Section 87l}3;regulation 18703.5.) "Immediate family,, is defined to
include the official's spouse and dependent children. (Section g2}2g)


File No. A-06-091
Page No. 9
However, you have not provided any facts which indicate a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Lutz'personal finances. Alone,
neither altemative you have proposed would automatically bar Councilmember Lutz from
making, participating in making, or influencing the govemmental decision by the COGS
to provide employee/consultant or the decision by the City to provide employee with
reimbursement from the COGS.
Ouestion 5
a
You have also asked whether CouncilmemberLltz would be prohibited under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or
influencing a decision of the RWQCB regarding the COGS, any member city, and,/or the
City. However, as you have not identified any particular governmental decision the
Commission can onlyprovide general guidance.
Neither proposed alternative would automatical ly bar Councilmember Lutz from
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision by the RWeCB
regatdingthe COGS, any member city, and/or the City as long as the decision would not
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the councilmember,s other
economic interests including her interest in her personal finances. If additional guidance
is needed regarding the applicability of the "personal financial effects" rule to any
particular govemmental decision, it is advisable that CouncilmemberLutzseek further
Commission advice providing all relevant facts.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
.-*'{ -e
(-'*) (-J--*-
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures

nor 'contributions' and should not be subject to limits. The bill does,

below are satisfied:

candidate.

elective office. The following tlpes ofpayments are presumed to be for

case it may be considered a gift under the provisions of Sict ion 8202g.

neither a gift nor a contribution ....,,

RWQCB...." The COGS and consequentially the member cities have offered to pay for
that assistance. All indications are that the COGS made such offers in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with Councilmember Lutz or at her request or
suggestion. Accordingly, we conclude that the payments to provide an
employee/consultant to assist Councilmember Ltilz *.r" -ud" at her behest. (Section
8201 5; regulation 1 8225.7 .)
You have presented two alternatives. Under the first altemative, the member
cities will pay for an additionally COG employee or a consultant to assist with
CouncilmemberLttz' services on the RWQCB. Under the second alternative, the City
will hire an employee to assist with Councilmember Lutz' services on the nWeCB and
the member cities of the COGS would reimburse the City. You have also provided the
facts that the employee/consultant will work solely on RWQCB matters and that no
funding will come from private or non-governmental entities. While the alternatives you
have proposed differ in form, the legal obligations are the same under both altematives
with the exception of identifring the payors of the payments, for reporting pu{poses, as
discussed below.
Based upon the facts submitted, the payments from the COGS, the mernber cities,
or the City, all local governmental agencies, will be received by ao employee/consultant
to provide services solely related to Councilmember Lutz' duties on the RWeCB. While
the occasional assistance of a professional experienced in the subject matter oith.
RWQCB would undoubtedly aid CouncilmemberLurrzin performing her duties, the
principal purpose of the assistance would be aiding and facilitatirg gi,,"--ental duties,
a govemmental purpose, and not principally for a personal putpose.
As the payments will be made by a local govemmental agency,the payments are
presumed to be for a purpose unrelated to the candidate's candidacy for et"Lt"a office and
are not considered contributions under section 82015@)Q)@Xii).3 Additionally, section
82015@)(2XBXiii) states that apayment by any entity, not just govemmental entities and
nonprofit orgarizations, made at the behest of an elected official principally for
legislative, govemmental, or charitable purposes will not be considered a contribution or
a gift as long as section 820r5(b)(2xB)(i) does not apply. (KampAdvice Letter, No.
A-97-63(a).) Therefore, we conclude that as the payments are principally for a
govefiImental purpose, the payments are neither contributions nor gifts under section
820L5(b)Q)@Xiii).. Please note that even though payments are neither contributions nor
gifts to CouncilmemberLltz, it is still important to caution that this conclusion isfact
sensitive.
Ouestion 3
Even though the payments made at Councilmember Lutz'behest do not appear to
be contributions or gifts, there is still limited reporting of these payrnents as the payments
will be made principally for a governmental purpose. (Section szbrs6;(2)(B)(iii), copy
3 We note that lgction 8201 s(bX2XB)(ii) also applies to payments by a non profit organization
orgarized under section 501(c)(3) ofthe Internal Revenue Code.
a Section s2015(b)(2)id)(iii; proviaes that payments which are not principally for personal
putpose made principally for a legislative, governmental, or charitabl" pr.por" are neitieru gift ro. u
contribution. As the classification of the payments is controlled by seciion 8201 5(bX2XBXfi), no further
sections.analysis:
of the palments is warranted under section 82028. (See Sutton Advice I-eier,'No.'a -OS-ZSA;
Taylor AdviceLetter, No. 4-03-085; Sellers Advice Letter,No. I-00-138; and SchmritAdvice Letter, No.
A-97-14s.)
enclosed.)s The payments to the employee/consultant hired for Councilme mber Lutz
must be reported as follows:
"... payments of this type that are made at the behest of a candidate
who is an elected officer shall be reported within 30 days following the
date on which the payment or payments equal or exceed five thousand
dollars ($ 5,000) in the aggregate from the same source in the same
calendar year in which they are made. The report shall be filed by the
elected officer with the elected officer's agency and shall be a public
record subject to inspection and copying pursuant to the provisions of
subdivision (a) of section 81008. The report shall contain the following
information: name of payor, address of payor, amount of the payment,
date or dates the payment or payments were made, the name and address
ofthe payee, a briefdescription ofthe goods or services provided or
purchased, if any, and a description of the specific purpose or event for
which the payment or payments were made. once the five thousand
dollars ($ 5,000) aggregate threshold from a single source has been
reached for a calendar year, all payments for the calendar year made by
that source must be disclosed within 30 days after the date the threshold
was reached or the payment was made, whichever occurs later. within 30
days after receipt of the report, state agencies shall forward a copy of these
reports to the Fair Political Practices Commission, and local agencies shall
forward a copy of these reports to the officer with whom elected officers
of that agency file their campaign statements.,, (Section
8201s(b)(2xBxiii).)
Under your first alternative, the member cities will pay the COGS to hire the
anployee/consultant. Under your second alternative, the City will pay aiemployee and
be reimbursed by the member cities. Under the second alternative,lt is unclear whether
the member cities will make payments through the COGS or directly to the City. These
scenarios create a question as to identi$ring the payors, which must be identified when
reporting the payments.
The Commission has advised that pa5rments from a nonprofit organization
aggtegating $5,000 or more in a calendar year must be reported where tLe organization
has made the payments from funds collected from individual donors. AdditiJnally,
payments from the individual donors must be reported if a donor has made payments
s The Commission has advised that where payments are made principally for legislative,
goverunental, or charitable purposes and equal or exceed $5,000 in the aggregate in a .ul"ndry year, then
reporting obligations will occur under section 82015(bX2XBXiii). (Kamp Advice Letter, supra; see also
Garvey Advice Letter, No. ,{-99-250.) Similarly, as the payments made at Councilmember^Lutz, behest
are from a govemmental agency, the payments will not be considered contributions or gifts, but the
payments are subject to the reporting obligations of section 92015(bX2XBXiii).
aggregating $5,000 or more in a calendar year to the nonprofit organization. (See Sutton
Advice Letter, supra; and Taylor Advice Letter, supra.)
Accordingly, Councilmember Lutz must report all parties who make payments
aggregating $5,000 or more in a calendar year. For example, if the member cities each
make payments aggregating$5,000 or more to the COGS; and the COGS use these funds
to make reimbursement payments to the City forpaylng acity employee to assist with
councilmember Lutz' services on the RwecB, each mernb er city,the sGVCoG, the
GCCOG, and the city are all considered payors and must be reported.
Ouestion 4
Additionally, you ask whether CouncilmemberLutzwould be prohibited from
making, patticipating in making, or influencing any govemmental decision under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act as a result of the coGS providing an
employee/consultant to assist with Councilmember Lutz' service on the niVqCn.6
Section 871OO prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or rnor" of the
public official's economic interests. (Section g7L}3;regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whJher an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given goveflrmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).) However, your questio, concirns only step three of the
analysis and entails only the determination of whether Councilmember Lutz has an
economic interest, which may be disqualifying.
step Three: what are the 66economic interests', of councilmember Lutz?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 1g703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition of
management (section 87 1 03 (d); regulation 1 3703. 1 (b));
6 Since the councilmember would not be receiving reportable gifts, it is not necessary to address
whether these payments are reportable under section g7207.


o
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section SZ1O:1";;
regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(e); regulation
18703.4);
a
a
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (section87103;
regulation 18703.5).
You have solelypresented the question of whether Councilmember Lutz would
have an economic interest in the COGS, the member cities and,lor the City should the
COGS, the member cities and/or the City provide an employee/consultani to assist with
CouncilmernberLutz' services on the RWeCB.
The only economic interests indicated from your account of the facts, is a possible
economic interest in the COGS, the member cities, or the City as a source of eifts and
Councilmemb er Llutz' economic interest in her personal finances.7 As previoirsly
discussed, the payments for an ernployee/consultant hired by a govemmental agency to
assist with Councilmember Lutz' services on the RWQCB would not be considereda
gift. Consequently, CouncilmemberLutzwould not have an economic interest in the
COGS, the member cities, or the City as a source of gifts under the facts provided.
As to the personal financial effects, a goveflrment decision has a personal
financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section SZtO:;
regulation 18703.5.) If facts suggest any financial effect on Couniilmember Lutz,
personal finances from a governmental decision, Councilmember Lrttz' economic interest
in her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the decision. (Regulation
187045.) A financial effect on an official's personal finances is considered material iq
"it is at least $250 in any 12-month period." (Regulation 18705.5(a).) If it is reasonabiy
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect of at least $250 in a l2-month period on
councilmemberLutz'personal finances resulting from her acceptance of the
employee/consultant's assistance, a conflict of interest violation.lvould occur.
7 As discrrssed above, an official's "personal finances" include the personal finances ofthe
official's immediate family. (Section 87l}3;regulation 18703.5.) "Immediate family,, is defined to
include the official's spouse and dependent children. (Section g2}2g)
However, you have not provided any facts which indicate a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Lutz'personal finances. Alone,
neither altemative you have proposed would automatically bar Councilmember Lutz from
making, participating in making, or influencing the govemmental decision by the COGS
to provide employee/consultant or the decision by the City to provide employee with
reimbursement from the COGS.
Ouestion 5
a
You have also asked whether CouncilmemberLltz would be prohibited under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or
influencing a decision of the RWQCB regarding the COGS, any member city, and,/or the
City. However, as you have not identified any particular governmental decision the
Commission can onlyprovide general guidance.
Neither proposed alternative would automatical ly bar Councilmember Lutz from
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision by the RWeCB
regatdingthe COGS, any member city, and/or the City as long as the decision would not
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the councilmember,s other
economic interests including her interest in her personal finances. If additional guidance
is needed regarding the applicability of the "personal financial effects" rule to any
particular govemmental decision, it is advisable that CouncilmemberLutzseek further
Commission advice providing all relevant facts.

QUESTION: 1. Would pa5rments by member cities of the San Gabriel Valley Council of
Govemments ("SGVCOG") and the Gateway cities council of Governments
("GCCOG"), referred to collectively herein as the "coGS," for the services of a
employee or consultant to provide staff services to Councilmember Lutz, solely to assist
with her services on the Los Angeles Regional Water euality Control Board
("RWQCB"), be considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember?
2. Would the assignment of a city employee to provide staff services to
CouncilmemberLttz by the City of Monrovia ("City') (reimbursed in whole or in part by
the member cities of the COGS) solely to assist with her services on the RWeCB be
considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember?
3. Would Councilmember L:utz be required to disclose the payments made for the
provision of staff services?
4. Would Councilmember Lttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing the COGS,
decision to provide employee/consultant or the City's decision to provide employee with
reimbursement from the COGS?
t Governm"nt Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appearat Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
qs:El
5. Would Councilmember Ltttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing a decision of
the RWQCB regarding the coGS, any member city, and/or the citt'l

CONCLUSION: 1 and 2. Based upon the facts submitted, the payments in question will be neither
reportable contributions nor gifts to the councilmernber.
3. While the payments in question would not be considered a contribution or a
gift, there is still limited reporting of these payments. CouncilmemberLytzmust report
any member city, the SGVCOG, the GCCOG, andlor the City if any of these parties
make payments aggregating $5,000 or more in a calend ar year.
4 and 5. Based on the facts submitted, Councilmember Lutz would not have an
economic interest in the COGS, the member cities, or the City as the payments in
question would not be considered a gift. CouncilmemberLttz,by virtue of the
payrnents, would not be prohibited by the Act from making, participating in making or
influencing a govenrmental decision, provided there is no personal financial effect on her
or members of her immediate family.

Would pa5rments by member cities of the San Gabriel Valley Council of Govemments ("SGVCOG") and the Gateway cities council of Governments ("GCCOG"), referred to collectively herein as the "coGS," for the services of a employee or consultant to provide staff services to Councilmember Lutz, solely to assist with her services on the Los Angeles Regional Water euality Control Board ("RWQCB"), be considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember? 2. Would the assignment of a city employee to provide staff services to CouncilmemberLttz by the City of Monrovia ("City') (reimbursed in whole or in part by the member cities of the COGS) solely to assist with her services on the RWeCB be considered a contribution or a gift to the councilmember? 3. Would Councilmember L:utz be required to disclose the payments made for the provision of staff services? 4. Would Councilmember Lttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing the COGS, decision to provide employee/consultant or the City's decision to provide employee with reimbursement from the COGS? t Governm"nt Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appearat Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations. qs:El File No. 4-06-091 Page No. 2 5. Would Councilmember Ltttz be prohibited under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act from making, participating in making, or influencing a decision of the RWQCB regarding the coGS, any member city, and/or the citt'l CONCLUSIONS 1 and 2. Based upon the facts submitted, the payments in question will be neither reportable contributions nor gifts to the councilmernber. 3. While the payments in question would not be considered a contribution or a gift, there is still limited reporting of these payments. CouncilmemberLytzmust report any member city, the SGVCOG, the GCCOG, andlor the City if any of these parties make payments aggregating $5,000 or more in a calend ar year. 4 and 5. Based on the facts submitted, Councilmember Lutz would not have an economic interest in the COGS, the member cities, or the City as the payments in question would not be considered a gift. CouncilmemberLttz,by virtue of the payrnents, would not be prohibited by the Act from making, participating in making or influencing a govenrmental decision, provided there is no personal financial effect on her or members of her immediate family. FACTS Councilmember Lutz is one of five members of the Monrovia City Council. In 2005, CouncilmemberLutz was appointed to the Los Angeles Regional Water euality Control Board. Many of the matters, which come before the RWQCB, have far-reaching policy
